data.title: S&P500: Tactical Buy - The Coiled Spring Trade (Upgrade)
data.description:
The 20% selloff

The S&P500 (SP500) closed below the 5000 level, nearly -20% below the all-time high. Thus, the S&P500 is on the edge of an official bear market.

Let's review the selloff timeline and the triggers - it's all about the tariffs.

  • The first 10% correction since February 21st was due to the soft data in the PMI and ISM surveys which suggested that businesses and consumers were getting concerned that tariffs would cause lower growth and higher inflation.
  • The tariffs were announced on April 2nd - and before the announcement and after the first indication that tariffs could be limited only to 10% universal tariffs, the stock markets were rebounding from the 10% correction, pushing on the critical 200dma resistance.
  • However, the tariffs announced were worse than the worst-case scenario, focusing on eliminating trade deficit with punitive tariffs on each trading partner, in addition to the minimum 10% tariff.
  • Thus, the next 5% selloff on Thursday was in response to the punitive tariffs - the day after the announcement.
  • The subsequent 5% selloff was due to the China's retaliatory tariffs on Friday.
  • Many expected that if there were no indications of trade de-escalation over the weekend, the markets could crash on Monday. There were no de-escalations, and the S&P500 was lower by over 5% in the futures market overnight, and opened lower.
  • However, during the trading day on Monday, there was a false rumor that the US would delay te tariffs by 90 days - and this sent stocks soaring with nearly a 10% intraday swing from the bottom to the top. This was the so-called coiled spring bounce, which faded. However, the S&P500 finished nearly flat for the day.
  • The action on Monday tells us that slightly positive news on trade could send the stock market soaring higher.
  • In fact, the Monday's positive action continued on Tuesday, with the S&P500 up over 4%, mostly due to the trade negotiations between Japan and the US. This confirms that if the current trade war evolves into negotiations with eventual tariff reductions, the stock market could soar higher.
  • The Tuesday rally was "killed" with the US announcing additional 50% tariff on China due to China's retaliatory tariff - this was the third selloff. However, this selloff was more muted, and the S&P500 closed above Monday's intraday lows, and above the bear market threshold. In addition, VIX (VXX) closed below Monday's high.
  • Thus, the question is whether Monday's low will hold as the bottom over the near term?

Here is the S&P500 chart showing the unfolding "crash", and the well-defined range 4800-5250.

Here is the VIX chart showing that peak fear was possibly on Monday.

What's next?

From this point the S&P500 will either enter a bear market with the selloff in excess of 20%, or we will get a major bounce to at least the 5500 level, and possibly higher.

The bearish case

The bear market at this point will have to be triggered by the data suggesting an imminent recession. The recent March labor market report does not suggest that we were in a recession in March, but this was before the tariffs were implemented. Thus, in my opinion, the current selloff will continue once we get the evidence that the labor market is slowing, which makes the weekly claims data released each Thursday extremely important. At this point, the weekly claims do not support the recessionary bear market.

In addition, the corporate earnings have to contract to justify the further selloff, and the Q1 earnings season is unlikely to show a contraction in earnings. However, corporate guidance will be important if corporations significantly slash their projected revenues. The upcoming earnings season over the next few weeks will be important.

The bullish case

This entire selloff was triggered by the tariffs, and President Trump is in full control - meaning he can stop this selloff at any point, and trigger a massive rally.

It is unreasonable to expect that President Trump would trigger a recessionary bear market, which could lead to the housing bubble crash and consequently cause a real financial crisis.

Thus, given that the selloff in excess of 20% would be dangerous for the real economy, it reasonable to assume that the 20% threshold could be the near-term bottom. This might be the level at which the Trump put is activated.

The fact that Tuesday's selloff was relatively modest and the S&P500 closed above the Monday's intraday lows is positive - given the horrible news of 104% tariffs on China.

In fact, the 104% tariff on China might be the worst news in this trade war cycle. In my opinion, we are transitioning to the negotiations stage, and we will get some positive news from these negotiations, especially with Japan and South Korea. Given the China 104% tariff, the EU is unlikely to retaliate, which is also a positive news.

Implications

I'm going tactically Long on the S&P500 (SPX), after covering the short position.

What does this mean? For me, tactically Long means I expect a sharp rally from the 20% threshold level. In fact, the rally could be so sharp, that the S&P500 could bounce by 10% in one day - it could spike to the 5500 level in a day. That's the coiled spring bounce - and the market is ready for it.

Specifically, there are many shorts, like the trend-followers CTA who will be squeezed higher and forced to cover, and there are still FOMO traders who can't wait to jump into the market.

One man controls the market and many of his associates are begging for de-escalation. The fact that Elon Musk is publicly confronting Navarro is a positive sign and signals a possible de-escalation.

Now, being tactically Long is a trade, not an investment. That means it's relatively small, and it has a strict stop-loss in case it goes the other way. I still think we will have a recessionary bear market with the AI bubble bust, and a credit event, so I would not buy yet for long-term at these levels, given the still elevated valuations and uncertain earnings growth.

data.title: 2 BDCs To Sell Before They Announce Dividend Cuts
data.description:
Over the past week, the private credit and BDC sectors have experienced very steep losses that are similar to those registered by the S&P 500 (SPY). In fact, the BDC index (BIZD) is down by almost 15% on a YTD basis, which is actually slightly worse than for the SPY.

There are many obvious reasons for this.

First, private credit is nowhere near a safe haven asset, which is what is necessary to shield portfolio asset prices during times like these.

Second, the potential economic shock has increased the odds of the Fed making some interest rate cuts in order save the economy - i.e., lower base rates are inherently bad for BDC results.

Third, in case the things really go south for corporate businesses, then small and relatively leveraged companies that have assumed private credit to do LBOs or major M&As are, arguably, more exposed to financial distress risks.

Fourth, when the uncertainty in this high, the deal activity tends to stop, which will make it more difficult for BDCs to grow their portfolios.

All in all, the BDC environment has gone from bad to worse. Before the tariffs came into the spotlight, many BDCs were already struggling with yield-compression and unfavorable refinancings (i.e., rolling over their cheap fixed rate borrowings to market-level/higher interest rates).

So, the solution for long-term investors is to rotate out from speculative BDCs, placing the received capital into higher-quality names. Investors, who are already long strong BDCs such as Kayne Anderson BDC (NYSE:KBDC), Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (NYSE:MSDL) and MSC Income Fund (NYSE:MSIF) should not fall into the panic mode as they carry high margin of safety to keep the existing dividends alive, while preserving the underlying NAV.

However, divesting speculative BDC investments is, in my opinion, a worthwhile exercise to minimize the potential losses that could be magnified once these BDCs circulate their new earnings reports, which would embody some of my aforementioned headwinds.

Plus, I think that the probabilities for many of these "junk BDCs" cutting their dividends have really surged higher now. So, this could also be the right moment to exit before the dividend reductions take place.

Below, I discuss two concrete BDCs, which I would avoid.

#1 - PennantPark Investment Corporation

PennantPark Investment Corporation (NYSE:PNNT) is the third most leveraged BDC, carrying a debt to equity of 1.56x. In my view, we could already stop here and conclude the case with a clear "no-go".

But of course, let's dive deeper into the business and fundamentals to understand a bit bigger picture.

To quickly expand on the leverage aspect: having this aggressive debt on the balance sheet (while the sector average is around 1.2x) means at least the following:

  • No room for additional growth.
  • High sensitivity to a single non-accrual.
  • High sensitivity to lower interest rates.

In my view, a conservative balance sheet is, arguably, the most important aspect to have for BDCs to not only minimize the effects from the new headwinds, but also to achieve some level of growth that could offset the forthcoming pressures.

PNNT does not have this advantage (i.e., it has the extreme opposite case).

To make things worse, let's consider these additional items.

In Q4, 2024, PNNT registered a core net investment income of $0.20 per share, which was $0.04 below the base dividend of $0.24 per share. So, PNNT is already in an unsustainable dividend coverage territory.

Furthermore, about 5.5% of PNNT's top-line stems from payment-in-kind proceed that are non-cash items. This percentage is higher if we look at the core NII level. Hence, the actual (cash-driven) core NII per share is lower than $0.20, thereby implying an even bigger gap between the dividend and earnings.

Speaking of PNNT's top-line, we have to also consider the fact that 38% of total revenue comes from a co-owned subsidiary, which is way more indebted than the BDC itself. The combination of the third most aggressive balance sheet and a highly leveraged subsidiary that is so material sound like a perfect recipe for a disaster.

Finally, approximately, 26% of PNNT's total exposure is tied to preferred share and equity investments, which are there mostly because of restructurings of PNNT's non-performing debt financings.

Theoretically, they could bring in sizeable cash flows, but it the current environment it seems highly unlikely that BDCs will manage to successfully monetize their equity stakes (especially, such low quality as we have in PNNT's case).

In a nutshell, PNNT is definitely a BDC, where I simply do not see any meaningful reason to allocate capital (unless the objective is to speculate for potentially juicy gains). Given the above, I also see a very significant probability of PNNT announcing a dividend cut this year.

#2 - FS KKR Capital

FS KKR Capital (NYSE:FSK) is not as bad PNNT. The BDC has definitely better fundamentals than those of PNNT. I was also fully invested in FSK before we entered 2024 and managed to lock in huge profits in January 2025. The reason for taking the gains home was related to valuations that had become a bit too rich relative to the underlying fundamentals. Plus, FSK was (and still is) certainly not among the highest quality BDCs, where it would make sense to assume a long-term buy and hold strategy.

In my humble opinion, FSK is very likely to cut its dividend, which, currently, yields ~15.5%.

I will give the key reasons why I think so.

First, based on the Q4, 2024 adjusted NII per share of $0.66, the base dividend coverage is 103%. If we include the supplemental dividends, the dividend coverage drops to 94%. It is clear that the total dividend will disappear (and soon), which is totally fine as these kinds of distributions are inherently volatile. Yet, I would argue that the base dividend is unsustainable as well. The base dividend coverage ratio of 103% does not indicate any meaningful margin of safety. It takes just one more non-accrual, one interest rate cut or unfavorable refinancings for FSK to fall into a level, where it would have to use leverage to fund the distributions.

Second, in Q4, 2024 FSK was forced to recognize two new non-accruals, which is a strong confirmation that the portfolio has not yet been cleaned up (even though in Q3, 2024 there were some positive indications of potentially fully solved issues). Against this kind of a market situation and the presence of almost no margin of safety, having suboptimal portfolio quality is a huge risk.

Third, FSK has PIK-paying loans, which as of Q4, 2024 consumed 12% of the total income. On an adjusted NII basis, the PIK income accounts for about 20% of the result. So, in reality, FSK is already using its credit facility to cover the existing dividend (on a cash basis).

Finally, this year FSK has two unpleasant borrowings to roll over, which had been assumed at below market-level rates. In 2026, there will be an additional $1 billion loan (at 3.4%) to refinance. This will introduce additional material headwinds for FSK.

The bottom line

BDCs have got hammered. Even though I am a BDC-bull, I think that the recent price declines that we have seen in this space are largely justified.

The risks have increased in the system, where it makes sense to factor in higher corporate bankruptcy rates. The odds of the Fed cutting interest rates have increased as well. The M&A and LBO market recovery has become questionable.

All of these aspects do not support BDCs. Instead, they introduce additional headwinds.

While I think that high-quality BDCs still remain protected, I have become even more skeptical about "speculative" BDCs that do not have the necessary margin of safety.

PNNT and FSK are two great examples of where, in my opinion, prudent investors should not invest.

data.title: Ituran: 6G, AI And Big Data Are Its Growth Engine
data.description:
As of writing this, markets have crumbled to COVID-19 levels. Tariffs are here, and almost all stocks are down. However, there is a glimmer of hope with this telematic stock that is based in Tel Aviv, Israel: Ituran Location and Control (NASDAQ:ITRN). The company uses technology that has been developed for the Israel Defense Forces (IDF). As we all know, IDF's technology is one of a kind: complex, precise, and efficient. Ituran is a telematics SAAS company that provides geological services for stolen vehicle recovery, fleet recovery, and connected car solutions. When you look at its subscriber growth, ITRN has a total of 2.4 M subscribers, representing a CAGR of 12% since its inception in 2004, when it began with 263 subscribers. Out of these 2.4 subscribers, 70% of them are recurring subscribers. The global automotive telematics market is expected to grow from $9 Bn in 2024 to $16.1 Bn by 2030 at a 10.1% CAGR. With that, I believe ITRN is well positioned to capitalize on this growth, given it has over 20 distributor centers in over 20 countries.

6G and AI as a growth engine

Since the introduction of the 5G networks in many cities around the globe, the telematics market has grown significantly. There is an increase in demand for connected services globally. As we move into 6G networks and AI technology, the telematics industry is expected to grow significantly. While the use of telematics devices is mainly used in premium and luxurious cars, the sales of these cars are projected to increase by 35% by 2030. And ITRN is taking advantage of this growth, especially with their collaborative partnerships with Porsche (OTCPK:POAHY) and Microsoft (MSFT) to bring real-time telemetry to the Porsche Carrera Cup, as noted by the CEO:

Motorsports represent the cutting-edge of what is possible in 2024 and the fact that Ituran technology is the heart of the Porsche racing car telemetry system demonstrates our technological leadership.

The commercial telematics market looks promising. With the introduction of AI and 6G, there is an expected surge in telematics subscriptions. In fact, according to ABI research, there will be over 126 Mn telematics subscriptions by 2027, growing at a CAGR of 13.2% between 2022 and 2027, as shown below:

Subscriptions 2022 Revenue Subscriptions-2027 Revenue
Asia-Pacific 27.3 Mn $9.6 Bn 57.3 Mn $18.9 Bn
North America 14.9 Mn $5.6 Bn 24.4 Mn $8.4 Bn
Western Europe 10 Mn $2.9 Bn 15.2 Mn $5Bn

This growth can be shown by the recent validation of Daimler India Commercial Vehicles for its connected telematics services, as noted by Eyal Sheratzky, CEO:

Daimler India Commercial Vehicles is a key strategic customer for our growing India operations. The successful validation of our products with DICV, reflects an important growth milestone for our JV with Lumax in that market. It expands our reach and reinforces Ituran's key position in the global connected vehicle market.

As of the latest earnings report, the company was adding an average of 157K subscribers per year, with the expectation that by 2025, the company would have added a total of between 180K to 200K subscribers. In my opinion, as long as the company continues with the development of new tech, then definitely there will be an increased demand for its products and services.

The Opportunity

Ituran was a Strong buy-rated stock up until February 28th. However, after posting their earnings report, the rating was affected after the company's growth prospects scored a D from an A-. As we have seen, while earnings have grown, forward EPS long-term growth of 9.40% performed below the sector median of 14.08%, which is one of the factors that contributed to the D score from a growth perspective. However, In my opinion, ITRN is positioned to achieve its target of 200K subscribers, which should improve the growth grade over time.

From a profitability point of view, ITRN ranks an A-, as a result of their solid margins. ITRN is trading at 508% and 150% from a TTM ROE and TTM EBITDA Margin perspective. This is in relation to their sector median, which clearly shows the huge discount that ITRN is trading at. As of 4Q 2024, their EBITDA margin had increased by 27.9% to $23.3Mn while net income had increased by 16.4% to $13.6Mn. In the bar graph below, ITRN is expected to double its current number of subscribers, assuming a CAGR of 12% y/y.

Ituran has a solid dividend policy. In Q4 of 2024, ITRN announced a cumulative dividend of $8Mn, bringing the cumulative dividend given out since IPO to a total of $336Mn. Additionally, ITRN was able to buy back shares worth $60 Mn. According to the CEO, ITRN would continue buying back shares and paying dividends. This is after the company completed the investment round in Bringg, which brought the company to a valuation of $1Bn. At the end of the day, these activities are meant to create and increase company value and, in the long run, should reflect in the share price.

Valuation

With regard to valuation, I would like to highlight the following:

  • Ituran is trading at a forward PEG ratio of 1.20, which represents an 11.05% discount in relation to its sector median.
  • In terms of forward EV/EBITDA, ITRN trades at 5.98x, which is a discount of 48.06% compared to the sector median.
  • From a forward P/E perspective, ITRN trades at 11.25x, which is a 37.01% discount to its sector median.
  • From a peer comparison point of view, ITRN is trading in line with its immediate peers. While ITRN is trading at 5.98x, CommScope (COMM) trades at 10.59x, Ribbon Communications(RBBN) at 6.34x, Adtran Holdings (ADTN) at 8.71x, while Netgear Inc. (NTGR) and Applied Optoelectronics (AAOI) trade at -7.69x and 9.605x, respectively. This makes me believe that Ituran is certainly undervalued and the market hasn't yet realized the intrinsic value of the company.

Ituran's Key Risks

The biggest risk for ITRN is currency volatility, as mentioned by the CEO during the release of the earnings report. While you'd expect that geopolitical instability, given the current war in Israel and Gaza, would be among the top risks affecting the company. However, this is not the case. In fact, the company has been performing extremely well during this period as crime rates and demand for ITRN's services have surged. Nevertheless, as the strength of the $ played a crucial role in holding back revenues and margins in the past years when yields were high, should the $ begin to weaken, its impact on the balance sheet should be meaningful.

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, Ituran is a great telematics company that is trading at a discounted value. Despite the steep decrease in share price over the last couple of days, I believe that the company has a lot more to offer. There is an anticipated growth in telematics and OEM services globally. And, as we have seen, there is growing demand. Lots of it! As people move to premium and luxurious cars, they will certainly need these products and services. And as cities and countries move into the era of 6G networks and AI, there will be a surge in demand for connected cars. As countries move to smarter cities, so will the demand for these products and services increase. ITRN has a solid and sustainable CAGR rate of 12%, which it has proven since its IPO. Its valuation is evidence that the company is trading at a discount, and the market is yet to realize its true intrinsic value.

data.title: The Fed Put Is Nowhere In Sight
data.description:
Betting on a sustained easing cycle is wishful thinking. However, despite what the headlines would have you believe, the outlook for the US economy is not all doom and gloom.

Inflation is still a problem for the Fed

The biggest headwind to a sustained easing of monetary policy has been, and remains, inflation. And while inflation has decelerated to a level to which the Fed has been able to initiate a number of rate cuts and effectively end QT, it remains far from levels which historically coincide with easy monetary policy.

Regardless of whether monetary policy is tight relative to inflation, we must remember all measures of inflation remain above the Fed’s 2% target.

Importantly, the stickiest measures of inflation to which the Fed is most concerned appear to be bottoming above said target.

If we delve a little deeper into its underlying components, we can see much of the deceleration of inflation over the past couple of years has been driven by the outright deflation seen on the goods side (despite goods only making up ~20% of the CPI basket).

Having said that, constructive progress has been made on the services front and for inflation overall. Such it was that a window presented itself to which inflation was still above target but largely a non-issue, allowing policymakers to ease. Unsurprisingly, they took advantage, as they should have.

It now seems as though this positive news is largely in the rear-view mirror. Upside inflation pressures look to be returning. Through February, Core CPI and Core PCE are growing at a three-month annualised growth rate of 3.5% and 3.9%, respectively. Well above target.

Barring a broad-based US recession, inflation is unlikely to fall below 2% for any sustained period of time, if at all. And while the odds of a US recession have risen in recent times thanks to Trump 2.0, it is not yet the base case. The most likely scenario is US inflation modestly trends higher from here, as the lead indicators are suggesting (particularly from a goods perspective).

These lead indicators are also yet to fully take into account any supply chain disruptions stemming from “Liberation Day”, so we still don’t know the full inflationary impacts of the newly introduced tariffs and reciprocal tariffs.

The most obvious effect will be felt on the goods front. So, with goods inflation lead indicators already trending higher, any further disinflation we may see on the services side is likely to be offset at the very least.

Consider the impact tariffs are going to have on used vehicle prices within the US thanks to the 25% tariffs on imported vehicles. Used vehicle inflation was one of biggest contributors to the post-COVID spike in inflation. While unlikely to reach the ~40% level seen in 2021, we are set to see another spike in this and similarly impacted areas of the good inflation spectrum.

How much of the impact of supply chain disruptions is offset by demand destruction remains to be seen, so assessing March and April data as it is released will provide much insight into just how challenged the outlook is for inflation.

It could well be that demand destruction wins the day, but for now, inflation lead indicators continue to modestly trend higher, so inflation is likely to follow suit. This is as good as it is going to get for the Fed on the inflation front.

For the jobs market, uncertainty abounds

Similar to the path of inflation in recent times, the US jobs markets has transitioned for secularly tight levels in 2022 and 2023 back to a state of normalcy.

Employment growth, job openings and weekly hours are all around average levels as of March’s data, while the unemployment rate of 4.2% is much closer to a cyclical low than a cyclical high.

The same can be said of wage growth.

Similar to inflation, this normalisation of the labour market has offered the Fed a window to ease monetary policy, such that the policy rate is roughly where it should be relative to employment growth.

The outlook for the labour market is where things get tricky.

For nearly a year now, I have been highlighting how employment lead indicators have bottomed and are turning higher, suggesting growth in some of the more cyclical areas of the jobs market (i.e., manufacturing and construction). That continues to be the case today, and if anything, employment lead indicators have accelerated over the past couple of months.

The problem is, much of the uncertainty surrounding DOGE and the impending public sector layoffs and second order job losses which will stem from a reduction in deficit spending (which could be as high as $1 trillion over the next 12 months) will show up in the jobs market at some point

That has yet to be the case. And again, as we can see below, employment lead indicators are unanimously moving higher. Most notably, we are yet to see a material spike in initial unemployment claims, which is where DOGE layoffs will first show up in the data.

Ultimately, DOGE could easily result in a 0.3-0.7% spike in the unemployment rate over the course of the year, so how much of this is offset by private sector job creation will be a key determinant of monetary policy during this period.

For now, employment lead indicators continue to point to a robust jobs market, meaning there is insufficient evidence to allow immediate easy policy in the immediate future. Leading indicators have never been so important.

The distribution of economic outcomes is widening

From an overall economic growth perspective, there remains much uncertainty, and uncertainty breeds an easier monetary policy bias. So, while US economic data is still robust, the outlook has deteriorated notably in recent weeks such that the distribution of potential economic outcomes in 2025 is as wide as it has been in years.

Should Q1 GDP forecasts prove true, impending economic weakness will provide the Fed with scope for rate cuts sooner rather than later. Now, as I explained in greater detail here, the Atlanta Fed’s real GDP Nowcast which informs the below is being skewed to the downside due to a large jump in gold imports, and is not necessarily yet reflective of an actual recession-like contraction in growth.

It is also worth highlighting some of the decline in Q1 GDP is also in part a result of the extreme cold seen throughout January and February in the US, which constrained economic activity, in addition to domestic firms front-running tariffs by (temporarily) boosting imports.

If we look at the key economic data points that inform most coincident growth indices more closely, we can see the US economy remains on solid footing through February and March data. Consumption, incomes and employment growth all remain around average levels, while industrial production ticked up over the winter months.

However, we are going to see some kind of deterioration in hard economic data in 2025. But similar to the employment story, it is the extent of this deterioration that will determine the path of monetary policy.

High-frequency economic data has modestly moved lower over the past month, but is not pointing to much downside in March’s hard data. It seems likely we will have to wait until later in Q2 for the potential slowdown in global trade, tariff uncertainty, and collapse in consumer and corporate sentiment to actually translate into slowing growth.

I suspect one of the biggest detectors from growth in 2025 will be the impending reduction in government spending. While DOGE will unlikely to able to reduce the US deficit by more than 10-15%, this still equates to a $500 million to $1 trillion reduction.

Government transfer payments to households are currently growing at their highest rate since COVID, so until the impending budget cuts are offset by corporate tax cuts and deregulation, there is a hole to which the US economy must tread.

What has also been a notable development in recent weeks is the potential end of the manufacturing recovery, which was on the cards in 2025. As we saw earlier, industrial production growth has ticked higher over the past quarter, in-line with the ISM Manufacturing PMI and short-term leads. The PMI has since moved back below 50 into contraction territory, while the Services PMI appears to be following suit.

Eventually, the deterioration is soft data will flow through to hard data, at least to some extent.

On a leading basis, it is important to reiterate we are not yet seeing a consensus decline in leading economic indicators that would generally precede a broad-based economic recession, only a modest slowdown.

Having said that, we are seeing short-term manufacturing lead indicators continue to roll over, as evidenced by the new orders and new orders/inventories indicators below. In addition, declining stock prices will translate into some form of negative wealth effect as we progress through 2025.

So, while a technical recession could ensure through the first half of 2025 similar to what occurred during 2022, lead indicators are still pointing to some form of robustness in the US economy, though the outlook is much more uncertain than it was only a couple of months ago.

Monetary policy is right where it should be

As it currently stands, monetary policy is where it should.

This is true based off hard economic data, but also true of surprises in both growth and inflation.

When we consider the above in context of the tightening of financial conditions (i.e. higher dollar and higher rates) that occurred in Q4 of 2024, such dynamics generally lead to downside surprises in growth and inflation. As such, the recent move lower in rates and the dollar is probably going to lead to some kind of upside surprises in hard economic data through Q2. This itself tends to be a headwind to monetary easing, but is of course a positive for risk assets.

When it comes to financial conditions, it is important to remember monetary policy remains relatively easy.

This is true whether we look at credit spreads or financial conditions indexes such as the Chicago Fed’s.

In all, monetary policy is broadly in line with where it should be based on current economic and financial data. With inflation unlikely to move lower, it would take a significant deterioration in the jobs market (which is still questionable), the economy (also questionable), or asset markets to warrant a sustained easing cycle from here.

There is a path to QE

However, though the outlook for rate cuts remains challenged, the avenue for a complete end to QT and potential QE appears an easier proposition.

What leads me to this conclusion is a combination of the following.

One, commercial bank reserves and the RRP balance at the Fed is closing in on multi-year lows. This is something the Fed pays close attention to.

Two, commercial bank cash assets are also closing in on multi-year lows. Both of these dynamics could become an issue given the dynamics surrounding debt issuance and demand for said debt. (I discussed this dynamic in greater detail here.)

And three, there is a significant level of public and private sector debt in need of refinancing in 2025. Again, as I detailed in my most recent outlook for the bond market, this is one of the primary risks facing markets in 2025:

There is roughly $3 trillion in Federal debt maturing in 2025, much of this short-term in nature thanks to the dynamics discussed earlier. That is a hell of a lot of debt that needs to be refinanced (some of which at higher interest rates), particularly if there is less scope to do so via short-term issuance and limited private sector funding capacity. And to make matters worse, there is an additional ~$2.5 trillion is corporate debt due for refinancing this year.

If this forthcoming maturity wall is not offset by expanding liquidity (via QE or a weaker dollar), it is likely to put significant pressure on funding markets as market participants will demand a higher interest rate and spread for taking on such risk.

The Fed will have issues cutting rates given their inflation targeting mandate, but QE is another means to provide the liquidity and weaker dollar required to meet the impending maturity wall.

So, unless the Fed is willing to abolish its arbitrary 2% inflation target, QE could be on the cards later this year, especially if risk assets continue to suffer.

Take the under on rate cuts

As it stands, markets are pricing in a 0.25% rate cut in July, another in September, and another in December.

That’s three cuts priced in during 2025, and four over the next 12 months. This is double what the market was pricing at the turn of the year, primarily thanks to Trump’s “Liberation Day” announcements and the chaos that has ensued.

As we have discussed, unless the Fed were to abolish its inflation target, the path to four rate cuts over the next 12 months would likely be one that is accompanied by a broad-based recession and significant rise in unemployment. Yes, growth is going to slow and unemployment rise, but evidence of a true recession is not yet presenting itself.

As such, I would take the under on four cuts over the next 12 months. One or two cuts this year, coupled with the full abolishment of what is left of the Fed’s QT program, seems a more likely outcome.

Key macro summary and outlook

- Due to historically strong household and corporate balance sheets, persistent fiscal support, lax immigration and labour hoarding, the US economy has continued to be resilient over the past two years, despite a multi-decade high in interest rates.

- As a result of these dynamics, households and corporations have been able to weather the storm of rising interest rates, leading to an income-driven business cycle.

- Despite recent weakness in employment and incomes growth, in addition to a downturn in the manufacturing sector, the US has avoided recession thanks to robust consumption.

- The strength of the US dollar and rise in interest rates over the final quarter of 2024 is now translating into downside surprises in economic growth, which are being accompanied by Trump 2.0 tariffs and impending government job cuts. This is creating a growth and stock market scare, with the latter being priced to perfection.

- Economic lead indicators are holding up okay for now, but we are seeing a deterioration in the data, particularly the soft data. DOGE budget cuts, increased uncertainty and inflation fears are going to translate into slower growth (less business investment, reduced consumption), but it is unlikely to lead to a broad-based recession.

- While private sector employment lead indicators are also modestly inflecting higher, DOGE is likely to lead to budget cuts of around $500b-$1t, which in turn is likely to push unemployment higher by around 0.3-0.7%.

- Trump wants a US economy that is not reliant on government spending, and is instead driven by the private sector. Roughly 95% of spending comes from defence, Medicare, Medicaid, social security and interest costs, which means there will be difficulty cutting the budget in these areas. However, it is likely there is spending abuse within all sectors of government, even those politically protected. Until tax cuts are implemented, there is a fiscal hole facing the economy.

- With inflation and employment lead indicators also trending modestly higher, the Fed will have a difficult time easing monetary policy unless Trump persists with tariffs and is able to crash the US economy. It remains prudent to take the under when it comes to expected rate cuts, but at some point it seems likely QE returns in some fashion, particularly given the debt refinancing wall facing markets over the next 12-18 months. Perhaps we will see the Fed abolish its 2% inflation target.

- Slowing growth should bias yields lower in the short to medium term, but it remains difficult to argue for a persistent rally in bonds outside of a recession. The deficit problem isn't going anywhere, inflation will remain above target absent a recession, and foreign capital continues to pull away from US debt markets.

- Equity markets need to reprice in-line with this new macroeconomic regime. I suspect this will unfold over the course of 2025.

Biggest risks facing markets in 2025

- The circa $3t in public debt refinancing requirements in 2024. There is also a notable amount of corporate debt maturing in 2025, following the terming-out of liabilities by the public and private sector at the secular low level of interest rates in 2020 and 2021.

- Trump wants to bring down bond yields, and the easier means of doing so may be crashing the stock market.

- The US natural gas market is set to face a historic rise in demand later in 2025 as the LNG export capacity comes on-line. This could significantly impact growth and inflation in 2026.

- Continued strength in the US dollar will continue to crowd out the public sector, as foreigners will sell US debt on net.

- The repatriation of global capital out of US equity and bond markets back to domestic markets. The great rotation appears imminent.

data.title: Apple: You Wanted It Cheaper, Now You Got It
data.description:
Just when we thought that the market was going to “suffer” after we dropped into a bear market, the market stunned those bearish prognosticators as the unfolding early market rally on April 8 reminded that even a dose of optimism on impending trade deals could spur a significant bottom, although the initial euphoria was wiped out toward the close, as investors took more risks off the table, in anticipation of the next phase in President Trump's reciprocal tariffs.

While last week the market digested the announcement of Trump’s reciprocal tariffs from his world-changing Liberation Day, this week, the Trump administration has returned to a deal-making posture, as world leaders are reportedly seeking to strike “good deals” with the US government, as they bid to undo the damage from Trump’s punitive tariffs regime that could potentially hurt the major US trading partners “badly.” Hence, the so-called tit-for-tat trade war has been limited to China (for now), even as Trump doubled down on his threat to impose additional tariffs on China’s decision to retaliate against the 34% tariffs set by the US last week.

Apple was already dealing with headwinds related to its lack of AI breakthroughs and snafu in its AI integration, as I discussed in my previous Apple update in February. Yet, things have quickly evolved into trade war dynamics dominating investor sentiments over the past one week, as Trump upped the tariffs ante, moving beyond mere negotiating rhetoric into real-world imposition (which could lead to an implosion if the US government isn't careful).

For Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL) (TSX:AAPL:CA) investors, perhaps, the apparent lack of a brutal and broadening global trade war (as yet) has likely calmed some nerves, even as investors assess the damage inflicted on Apple’s supply chain in Asia.

Make no mistake about it. Even though Apple has diversified its supply chain to India and Southeast Asia, its China manufacturing base managed by its contract manufacturers remain as Apple's primary outpost, suggesting if Trump really does fulfill his pledge to hit back with more import taxes on China on April 9, it could trigger another moment of reckoning for AAPL investors, after an unceremonious drop to lows last seen in April 2024.

Analysts have already started to worry about the hit to Apple’s margins, even as it boasts a best-in-class gross margins profile, and also wields substantial branding power to mitigate any potential impact with increases in prices for its products. Moreover, it must also be highlighted that Apple has already diversified its business model with its higher-margin services segment.

As seen above, services accounted for about 40% of Apple’s overall gross profits, rising from 36% over the previous year. Hence, it goes to show that Apple has been able to monetize its solidly profitable services segment (74.4% gross margins on a last twelve months basis), corroborating the robustness of its wall garden strategy.

In addition, Apple has also made plans to diversify its iPhone production out of China into India, given the lower tariff rates, amid the possibility that a deal might be struck with Apple’s supply chain partners based outside of China.

Words out this April 8 morning as I pen this article indicates that the Trump administrations seems to be making constructive progress with its Asian trading partners, as touted by administration officials, suggesting that things may not be as gloomy as we think it is (although negative surprises cannot be downplayed).

Moreover, Wall Street is already downgrading their near-term estimates on Apple, suggesting expectations are decidedly low when we hear from CEO Tim Cook and his team as we close in on its earnings conference on May 1.

And you know what, let’s not rule out the possibility that Apple may also be granted some kind of reprieve from President Trump, as Cook works behind the scenes to lobby and “enrapture” the Trump administration after a successful record in Trump 1.0, particularly as Apple stands out with its $500B multi-year investment figure in the US.

Yet, that thesis was put to the test on April 8, as the White House insists that President Trump believes Apple can manufacture the iPhone (Apple’s most critical product revenue driver) in the US, although Trump's press secretary didn’t mention whether that is even financially viable, as it could decimate Apple’s margins, making such a move arguably a non-starter to even begin with.

Hence, the initial euphoria of an AAPL stock rally in the morning of April 8 gave way to another stumble by the end of the day as it fell 5% to mark the market’s displeasure and anxiety over the trade negotiation likely going on between Cook and the Trump administration, while the impasse on a near-term trade deal with China looks set to be delayed further.

Hence, while the China supply chain remains a palpable risk that could unravel if Cook couldn’t achieve an exemption or if China comes down hard on Apple’s decision to further diversify its supply chain, AAPL’s valuation premium might have room to fall, particularly since the stock is likely viewed more defensively by investors, given its solidly profitable ecosystem, previously stable supply chains, and a defensible iOS walled garden that continues to expand its services revenue stream, lifting its overall gross profitability given the pretty wide bifurcation in product and services margin.

Despite downgrading Apple’s outlook, analysts still see a continued growth evolution in Apple’s gross margins, which in turn bolsters its operating margins over the next year.

Of course, I understand that things may change as ongoing developments on the tariffs situation evolve, implicating Apple’s supply chain resilience. Yet, it has also demonstrated management’s foresight into reducing the concentration in its China manufacturing base, while diversifying into other nations at the same time.

While there’s no doubt that Apple’s product margins are expected to be impeded, its growing services margins and the opportunity to be granted certain exemptions by the Trump administration should help to underpin near-term optimism, if investors contemplate that the stock has already dropped far enough for them to consider picking up the pieces.

Sure, some may argue that AAPL isn’t priced anywhere close to a discount, and they might be right. Based on AAPL’s forward EBITDA multiple of almost 19x, it’s almost 65% above the sector median, although the case can be presented that it’s also more than 10% below its 5Y average.

In other words, AAPL is now trading at a relative discount when we consider its average valuation levels over the past five years. Bullish investors might also point out that if Apple is expected to get increasingly more profitable as it drives up its services growth over time, then there appears to be a discernible bifurcation against the current 5Y average, auguring well for a buyable opening into its recent pessimism.

As a prominent Magnificent Seven player in the S&P 500 (SPX) (SP500) and the Nasdaq (NDX) (QQQ), a potential bottom that could also occur quickly based on whether the event-driven (negative) catalyst could reverse more favorably back to the upside as President Trump possibly strikes more trade deals, AAPL will likely also benefit from the “rising tide” analogy, given its pivotal exposure to the market.

In addition, AAPL stock remains well-poised to bottom out above the 200-week moving average, which has remained in a resolute uptrend bias, and one that could get robust support from dip-buyers assessing an opportunity that they shouldn’t miss. Although the slide yesterday appears to have tempered the initial optimism, it has yet to decisively crater a potential bottom aligned with the 200-week MA, suggesting the risk/reward remains delicately poised for a nadir, even as pessimism abounds.

All eyes will be on President Trump doubling down on his threat to China this week, and how China might respond to already frail trade ties. However, AAPL’s price action and the lack of significantly bearish earnings downgrades from Wall Street suggests that the damage to AAPL could be limited. If Cook could achieve a welcomed reprieve from Trump, that could send the stock surging back up.

So, even as the “Apple panic” appears to have griped investors as I write this update, I believe the opportunity for investors who have been biding their time for a “grand” entry into AAPL shouldn’t miss out on buying this setup.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: CVRx Inc: Overreaction Creates Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Thesis Recap

I last wrote about CVRx Inc (NASDAQ:CVRX) with the thesis that the stock was very expensive and the company's top-line growth was priced in. Since my last article, the stock has fallen a dramatic 60%. Off of recent Q1 preliminary revenue falling short of expectations, I believe the stock market has overreacted and presents investors with a potential buying opportunity. The company still has the same product, total addressable markets, and excellent clinical track record of success. Shares are rated a buy with a price target adjusted downward to $10.

Focus On The Long-Term

I think markets are becoming extremely short-sighted these days. One bad quarter can send a stock down 40%, when nothing much has changed in terms of the company's long term trajectory. A statement of the company clarifies an upcoming weak quarter,

“While we continue to make significant progress in driving adoption of Barostim, first quarter revenue fell short of our expectations,” said Kevin Hykes, President and Chief Executive Officer of CVRx. “A core element of the commercial strategy initiated last year has been to build a world-class sales organization. As part of this effort, we brought in a number of new high-quality sales representatives in the back half of 2024 and the first quarter of 2025. We are thrilled with the level of talent we have attracted to strengthen our team, but many of these newer sales representatives are still in the early stages of territory development.

Completely understandable. The total addressable market is very large for CVRx Inc at $2.2 billion annual revenues according to their investor presentation. This hasn't changed at all, but the recent statement suggests that the company needs some time to build a world-class sales organization to chase its unsaturated market. Furthermore, the company explained that the first quarter of 2025 was impacted by seasonal softness as patients have yet to schedule their procedures and treatments yet. To me, this is purely temporary and a massive market overreaction to a minor bump in the road.

Furthermore, the numbers aren't too bad to me. The company still guided "Total revenue for the first quarter of 2025 is expected to be approximately $12.3 million, representing an increase of approximately 15% over first quarter 2024 revenue of $10.8 million". A 15% revenue increase YoY is still rather impressive, just shy of the previous expected $14.5-$15 million. We are talking about a miss of a few million dollars in one quarter, not billions for the full year. A 40% drop in the stock price is arguably one of the biggest overreactions I've seen here, as the fundamental story of the company hasn't changed.

To reiterate from my last article, the company has an extremely innovative, disruptive product known as Barostim. Approved by the FDA and heavily reimbursed, this product is designed to non-intrusively treat heart failure symptoms. Truly one of a kind, I had high praise of this company's efforts and clinical track record but felt the stock was too expensive before. By the numbers, Barostim has a 94% clinical response rate, 3x greater improvement in quality of life, and excellent functional status results with a near 66% improvement in patients according to their website.

Many people around the world suffer from heart complications. According to their presentation, the expected annual costs are $70 billion by 2030. Barostim is the answer to this problem, as management heavily reinvests in its sales team to chase after this big market. There are arguably no similar products on the market that utilize neuromodulation therapy to effectively treat heart failure symptoms that I am aware of today. I also find Barostim to be an easy sell to patients as it is non-intrusive, highly and immediately effective, and reimbursed by CMS so insurance typically covers it.

The bottom line is that this company has tripped, but hasn't fallen. Seeing the recent 40+% drop is quite ridiculous to me, as the company guided a slightly weak quarter. Not a year, but just a quarter. Investors who liked this company and its innovative product should consider buying at a discount here, when not much has changed in terms of the long-term fundamental story.

Average Selling Prices Can Increase

For those not aware, the Center for Medicaid Services has reimbursed Barostim therapy, which I see is a massive sign of confidence from the medical expert community. The reimbursement level has been maintained at $45,000 for 2025, suggesting confidence in the product is still strong. According to the earnings call,

In the final 2025 Hospital Outpatient Prospective Payment System Rule, CMS maintained Barostim in the new technology APC 1580 for 2025, preserving the current reimbursement level of approximately $45,000 for outpatient procedures. This decision, once again, appropriately recognizes the resource requirements associated with the Barostim implant procedure and supports continued patient access.

We are pleased that hospital reimbursement has now been effectively equalized between inpatient and outpatient settings for 2025, allowing clinicians to treat their patients in the most clinically appropriate setting independent of economic considerations.

Independent of economic considerations essentially means that it's basically free for hospitals to use Barostim. Barostim also got recently coded from Category 3 to Category 1 (effective January of 2026), formalizing and solidifying Barostim as a to-go options for physicians. All of this is excellent news, and I find the recent correction a good buying opportunity for believers of Barostim's commercial success in the market.

One note that I found interesting is that management's $2.2 billion TAM estimate is based off a conservative ASP estimate of $29,000. I find this to be rather low as the current reimbursement level is already at $45,000. I think this company has hidden pricing power, as they can effectively charge much more for this life-saving product. Patients who have insurance shouldn't have trouble affording Barostim, as it is covered under Medicare. Therefore, I actually think the TAM estimate is conservative and management can potentially increase prices while still making their product affordable to the general public.

When it comes to saving lives and helping people with heart failure, demand is arguably very inelastic. With limited competing options that have a successful clinical record, I argue Barostim is a one of a kind product that has significant pricing power. The ball is in management's court if they want to turbocharge sales, and their formation of a world-class sales team is likely to help drive shareholder value.

CVRX Stock Valuation - $10 Fair Value

I'm dropping my price target to $10 as the company is still unprofitable on a GAAP basis and is seeing some slowdown in their sales. While temporary, I prefer to be conservative in my projection estimates. Based off management's preliminary guidance, I will assume sales to be $12 million a quarter, leading to FY 2025 sales to be around $50 million ($12 million x 4 = ~$50 million), which is short of their previous guidance of $63-$65 million.

With no earnings or positive cash flow, I'll use a simple sales multiple of 5x to get me a market cap of $250 million. Divide by shares outstanding of 26 million gets me $10 fair value, rounded up for simplicity. Five times sales seems reasonable as it's conservatively below the 5Y average of 9x, and yet is a well-deserved premium above the sector median of 3x.

As an early-stage company with no GAAP profits yet, this company will take a while to become profitable. Nonetheless, I think the company's historically low share price is a buying opportunity as it discounts the company's progress in category coding, strengthened sales force, and its impressive TAM. A stock like this is best suited for investors who like early stage disruptors that aren't profitable yet, but can grow in the double-digits for many years to come. As a high-risk high-reward type investment, investors are betting on management's ability to capture a potential multi-billion dollar market and sustain a dominant market position. If successful, I think the payoff is potentially multiples to one.

Risks

New entrants can potentially disrupt and offer a better product that is superior to Barostim. Regulatory changes may make Barostim less attractive as new political leaders make changes in the Department of Health and Human Services. Their reliance on Barostim as a single product makes this an all-in or nothing type of investment, arguably not suitable for those who prefer conservative type investments.

Reimbursements can change. If the CMS stops reimbursing Barostim due to legal or clinical concerns, then this company can lose a lot of momentum. Furthermore, their sales team still has no track record yet of overcoming adoption barriers and may see disruption and personnel changes that slow down progress.

Dilution and stock-based compensation expenses are potential concerns. Investors may see their ownership diluted as the company relies on outside money to keep the business going. Excessively high and growing SG&A expenses are still a concern as they have grown to $91 million, eating the majority of current gross profits. It is uncertain whether their sales-related expenses will payoff in the long run.

Buy CVRx

As a high risk/high reward type of investment, this stock is best suited for believers in Barostim who are willing to suffer an all-in or nothing outcome. Barostim's commercial success may potentially lead to billions in sales, as the total addressable market is currently estimated to be $2.2 billion. However, failure to manage SG&A expenses, regulatory and reimbursement changes, and potential dilution may lead to significant losses. Nonetheless, fans of the company should see the recent drop as a buying opportunity and remain focused on the long term. Shares are a buy with a price target of $10.

data.title: Voya Index Solution Portfolios Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

These portfolios are only offered as an investment option within variable products and retirement programs.

You should consider the investment objectives, risks, and charges and expenses of the variable product and its underlying fund options; or mutual funds offered through a retirement plan, carefully before investing. The prospectuses / prospectus summaries / information booklets contain this and other information, which can be obtained by contacting your local representative or by calling (800) 992-0180. Please read the information carefully before investing.

Key takeaways

In the fourth quarter of 2024, U.S. stocks continued their upward trajectory following Donald Trump’s presidential victory. The U.S. bond market struggled due to concerns about sticky inflation, increased deficit spending, and a more conservative rate-cut path from the Federal Reserve, which saw the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield rise by more than 80 basis points (bp) to 4.58% by the end of the quarter. Global markets face challenges in 2025, but the U.S. remains attractive due to post-election optimism, investment, productivity gains, and energy independence, with the Fed expected to continue gradual easing. We continue to overweight U.S. large and mid-cap stocks, driven by strong corporate profitability and a resumption of corporate spending, while maintaining a neutral stance on U.S. investment-grade core bonds and an underweight in foreign currency denominated bonds.

The Portfolios posted negative absolute and relative returns for the period, underperforming their strategic allocation benchmarks on a net asset value (NAV) basis.

Market review

U.S. stocks continued their upward trajectory during the fourth quarter following Donald Trump’s presidential victory. The consumer discretionary and communication services sectors led, while materials and healthcare lagged. Large cap stocks outperformed small caps, and growth stocks significantly beat value stocks.

The U.S. bond market struggled during the quarter on concerns about sticky inflation, increased deficit spending and the U.S. Federal Reserve’s more conservative rate-cut path, as the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield rose by more than 80 bp, ending the quarter at 4.58%. The Fed cut rates by 25 bp in November and December. However, the central bank now projects just two rate cuts in 2025, reflecting a more cautious stance in response to strong economic data.

The U.S. election, marked by Trump’s proposed tariffs, increased global uncertainty, lead to further U.S. dollar strength and broadly weighed on international equities. Europe struggled with political instability in France and Germany and rising energy costs ahead of winter. In yen terms, Japanese equities rose due to strong corporate activity including strategic mergers, share buybacks and improved return on equity. Meanwhile, China dealt with weak consumer activity, property sector challenges and concerns about regulatory changes and economic restructuring. These combined factors caused international equities to continue to underperform.

Outlook

Global challenges and opportunities confront the markets in 2025. However, the U.S. remains attractive driven by post-election optimism, investment, productivity gains from deregulation, innovation and energy independence. Despite some softening, the U.S. labor market remains resilient. Inflation is expected to stay manageable, allowing the Fed to continue its gradual easing.

Supported by this positive outlook and President Trump’s pro-business proposals, we continue to overweight U.S. large and mid-cap stocks. Large-cap valuations appear expensive, but they are supported by strong corporate profitability. The increasing weight of the technology sector with its capital-light business models suggest that higher margins may be the new norm, making expectations more reasonable. With the election concluded, corporate spending is expected to resume and drive positive earnings growth as well. Mid-caps offer enticing valuations and are poised to benefit from a broadening expansion and lower borrowing costs and provide more financial stability than small caps.

Outside the United States, pockets of weakness are becoming more apparent, particularly in the eurozone and China. The eurozone faces cooling services and ongoing manufacturing challenges, heightening economic uncertainty. In China, new policies aim to address economic sluggishness, especially in the property market, and boost investor confidence. However, the investment environment looks more promising in Japan, where deflation has been curbed and corporate reforms have made progress. Despite this, there has been relatively little foreign investment, creating an opportunity as more investors recognize the growth story. Given this mixed picture, we continue to prefer U.S. equities.

In fixed income, portfolios are neutral on U.S. investment-grade (IG) core bonds, which offer decent yields and portfolio stability. In October, portfolio duration to a modest underweight. While conviction on rates near-term direction is limited, we believe core bonds will hedge against equity risk due to the transition from inflation to growth risk that has occurred. While credit stress and defaults are expected to remain controlled, high yield spreads are very tight, leading us to prefer equities for risk allocation. In addition, an expectation that the U.S. dollar rally will continue and a positive U.S. economic outlook justify a preference for U.S. assets and an underweight in foreign currency denominated bonds.

Positioning

At the beginning of the period, Portfolios were tactically overweight in U.S. mid-cap stocks and underweight in U.S. large-cap stocks. Emerging market (EM) equities were increased to remove an underweight by selling U.S. large cap and small cap. China’s recent enactment of monetary stimulus and its commitment to future fiscal measures indicate that policymakers are prepared to take action to meet their economic targets, which could provide a tailwind for EM stocks. In December, U.S. large-cap equities were reduced, and short-term bonds were increased. This move came after the post-election run-up and the elevated valuations, as we deemed it prudent to lock in some gains and diversify away from the momentum trade.

Overall, Portfolios continue to maintain a balanced posture with a preference for U.S. large and mid-cap equities, and core IG fixed income.

Performance

The Voya Index Solution Portfolios’ primary performance objective is to outperform its strategic allocation benchmark over the long term through tactical asset allocation, which involves making short- to medium-term changes in the asset allocation to benefit from temporary mispricings or market inefficiencies. The benchmark return is the weighted average return of indices that represent asset classes included in the strategic allocation benchmark. Index returns are gross of all fees. The Portfolios invest in passive index funds to gain exposure to asset classes. The Portfolios generally are rebalanced monthly and the strategic asset allocations are updated annually to reflect changes to our capital market assumptions. In the fourth quarter of 2024, Portfolios’ underperformed their strategic allocation benchmarks on a NAV basis. Tactical asset allocation was a detractor.

Tactical asset allocation had a negative impact on performance during the period. Portfolios’ tactical underweights to U.S. large cap and overweights to EM equities were the main detractor, as large U.S. stocks delivered another period of positive performance, helped by continued strong corporate earnings and despite sharply rising long-term interest rates. EM equities struggled due to geopolitical tensions, economic uncertainties in key regions and a stronger U.S. dollar.

The Voya Index Solution Portfolios are comprised of passive index funds. The funds may not perfectly track the performance of the underlying asset class benchmarks. During the quarter, index funds’ performance differences detracted.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: Buffett Was Right, Again
data.description:
Article Thesis

Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.A)(NYSE:BRK.B) has been a seller of equities in the recent past, prior to the most recent sell-off and market volatility. With a gigantic cash position, Berkshire Hathaway is well-positioned to hunt for bargains during the current sell-off—volatility could make for highly attractive buying opportunities for Berkshire Hathaway. Warren Buffett has positioned Berkshire Hathaway very well with his asset allocation decisions over the last year, and BRK shareholders could benefit a lot from that.

Past Coverage

I have written about Berkshire Hathaway here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently earlier this year when I covered the company's Q4 results. Despite the recent market sell-off, Berkshire Hathaway has risen marginally since my last article was published, showing, once again, BRK's stability and trustworthiness during times of crisis.

With Berkshire Hathaway being very well positioned for the current volatility and with its cash hoard becoming way more important during this environment, it is time to take another look at the Warren Buffett-led imperium today.

Buy Low, Sell High: Warren Buffett's Strategy

Warren Buffett has been a value investor for decades, thus one of his mottos is that investors should ideally buy when things are cheap, and that they should (potentially) sell when valuations are stretched. This seems like a pretty logical strategy, but there are other approaches as well—some growth investors do not mind valuations a lot, momentum investors sometimes buy into already high valuations if they believe these will expand further, and so on. Of course, Warren Buffett and many other value investors consider other factors as well when making buy and sell decisions, e.g. by looking at a company's quality—margins, balance sheet strength, and so on—and by accounting for growth potential. But still, all else equal, valuation matters—and that explains Warren Buffett's decisions to buy and sell Apple (AAPL) and other stocks at specific times.

Berkshire Hathaway's Apple purchases began in early 2016, when Apple's valuation was very low, as we can see in the following chart:

The chart begins in January 2016, thus around the time when Berkshire Hathaway started to build its position in AAPL. The company was trading at a low double-digits earnings multiple at the time, while its enterprise value to EBITDA multiple, which also factors in debt and cash on the balance sheet, was in the high single-digits. Towards the end of 2024, valuations were more than 3x as high, with Apple peaking at around 40x net profits and close to 30x EBITDA. In retrospect, Warren Buffett's timing with the beginning of his purchases was very good, and the sales of AAPL worked out well, too: In Q2 of 2024, for example, Berkshire Hathaway sold around $80 billion worth of AAPL stock. Looking at the price chart, Berkshire Hathaway began buying Apple at around $20 or so, while the massive selling in 2024 was done at around 10x that amount. AAPL stock peaked at $260 last year, but has fallen by around one-third since then, dropping to around $170 during the recent market turmoil. While Warren Buffett made some statements about his selling being driven by tax reasons (worries about a potentially higher capital gains tax in the future), I believe that there is a good likelihood that AAPL's very high valuation in 2024 played a big role, too—maybe the tax reasoning was a bit of an excuse to not scare the market.

Apple is not the only company where Warren Buffett's value investing approach allowed Berkshire Hathaway to sell down a position at a great time. The company sold around 40% of its Bank of America (BAC) position in the second half of 2024, where shares peaked at just above $48 and where the valuation rose to as much as 15x net profits—considering Bank of America trades at less than 10x forward net profits today, the valuation in the second half of 2024 was elevated. Selling down shares in the $40s worked out well, with BAC dropping to the mid-$30s since.

Berkshire Hathaway's Gigantic Cash Hoard

The above examples show that Warren Buffett's approach to value investing allows him to time entries and exits well, which naturally results in hefty trading/investing gains over time. On top of that, timing exits well—selling when valuations are high—allows Berkshire Hathaway to build up cash positions during times when valuations are stretched, and when there aren't a lot of attractive investments available. This is clearly showcased by the company's balance sheet as of the end of the fourth quarter:

We see that the cash position experiences ups and downs over time, with a huge increase in 2024—BRK's cash position doubled in a single year, rising to a gigantic $334 billion, which is equal to roughly one-third of the company's market capitalization today. While some investors, analysts and readers wondered what Buffett was doing with all this cash, and some questioned whether that was a smart idea, we now know that Warren Buffett was completely right: He increased Berkshire Hathaway's cash exposure prior to a big market sell-off, and is now very well positioned to pounce on investment opportunities during the current sell-off.

While we do not know yet what the ultimate impact of the current "tariff war" will be, we know that many stocks have become way cheaper over the last couple of weeks. The aforementioned Apple has dropped by around one-third, for example, and other big tech names have lost one-third or more since their recent highs as well, including NVIDIA (NVDA) and Meta Platforms (META). The S&P 500 index (SPY) is trading around 20% below its recent highs, meaning we are in a bear market now. Likewise, other indices such as the S&P 400 mid-cap index have declined by around 20%, too, and many individual stocks dropped more than that. Industries Warren Buffett is interested in, judging by past equity purchases, such as banking and energy, have declined even more than the broad market:

Among those industries, some individual stocks have fallen even more than the industry average, such as Citi (C), which has dropped by 31% from its 2025 high over the last couple of days. We do not know whether Warren Buffett is buying right now, or whether he is waiting for things to calm down, or whether he believes that we will see even more selling pressure—but he has the ability to expand its stock portfolio if he wants to. Berkshire Hathaway has a lot of flexibility, can wait for the best bargains to appear, and can pounce and make huge deals thanks to its enormous cash hoard—an enviable position for sure.

Is Berkshire Hathaway A Good Investment Now?

Berkshire Hathaway is in a very good position right now: Its huge cash hoard gives it a lot of options, and risks are low thanks to its internal diversification and thanks to many US-centered businesses, such as insurance, where the tariff threat is small.

But that does not automatically mean that BRK is a good buy right here—after all, the market accounts for Berkshire Hathaway's strong position, as BRK is far from cheap right here:

Berkshire Hathaway trades at a little more than 1.6x book value right now, using the end-of-Q4 book value number. Book value has likely declined a bit since then; thus the current book value is likely a bit higher than that. Considering where Berkshire Hathaway traded in the past, that is a rather elevated valuation—shares oftentimes traded at 1.2x to 1.5x book value.

So while Warren Buffett has positioned BRK well to benefit from the current sell-off, that doesn't mean that BRK itself is a bargain today—instead, it looks fully valued. In case that changes and shares come down during the current bear market, BRK could be an excellent investment, but at an above-average valuation, relative to its historic valuation range, I do not think that BRK is a must-buy today.

data.title: Where To Hide While The Shrapnel Flies?
data.description:
Introduction

This should have been a piece written when the markets were smooth, and everyone was making money. Unfortunately, for too many of us, we do not think about reservoirs of safety until it is too late. Since inauguration day, the S&P 500 has lost almost 16% of its value, and is a net negative gain since May 2024. My most recent piece discussed the necessity of having a well-diversified portfolio, but here, I want to go into more depth as to which asset classes generate positive gains during market upheavals.

Cash

What can I say more than one should hold a reserve of investment cash? Now, while one is still contributing to a retirement account, it is perfectly understandable that maintaining a 1% position in cash is normal. However, as one begins to approach retirement or is in retirement, the cash position needs to be at least equal to one’s anticipated cash expenses for the next 12 months or longer. This should never be invested money. Even the largest actively managed mutual funds maintain a cash position no larger than 15%. Currently, the average money market yield is 0.63%, but there are higher yields if one is willing to shop around. Since many money markets invest in short-term treasuries, here is how the Bloomberg Treasury Bill 1-3 Month Index has performed over the years.

Cash has demonstrated an ability to avoid losses, even during down markets. We are not going to beat the market with cash, but that is not the point. The goal is to manage our risk, and cash does that admirably.

Cash Equivalents

When it comes to cash equivalents, we are talking about certificates of deposits (CDs), which I prefer to keep in a 1–3-year timeframe. The goal here is to keep a basket of money safe from the ups and downs of the overall market so one can fund any near-term needs, i.e., the first few years of retirement. Using the Bloomberg Treasury Bond 1-3 Year Index, we can see we have a very safe investment approach.

While it is possible to lose money with this approach, usually due to a rising interest rate environment and selling before maturity, this is the appropriate approach for those wanting to manage risk. As I see it now, the best performing ETF that can act in the role of a cash equivalent is SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Mo T-Bill (ARCX: BIL) ETF. Since January 20, it has generated a total return of 0.86% and has a current yield (TTM) of 4.85%.

Investment Grade Bonds

Investment-grade bonds are those loans that receive a bond rating of BBB or higher. The bonds are debt instruments backed up by federal or municipal governments and corporations. Based on data from the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index, we find an investment class that outperforms the S&P 500 in every key risk category.

The S&P 500 generates a better return than bonds over 39 years. But when it comes to risk management, investment-grade bonds are fantastic investments. Bonds have a better probability of generating a positive return (1.26 v. 0.67), beating inflation (0.60 v. 0.59), holding even on beating the risk-free rate (0.57 v. 0.57), and controlling downside deviation (0.83 v. 0.76). While the worst 12-month period was not very fun, over time, bonds have shown an ability to average a positive return (5.21%) during down markets.

Based on current risk history, the best ETF is SPDR Bloomberg 1-10 Year TIPS ETF (ARCX: TIPX), which has returned 3.58% since inauguration. The current yield (TTM) is 3.42%.

International Bonds

The best available ETF for international bonds, FlexShares Ready Access Variable Income ETF (ARCX: RAVI), has returned 0.94% since January 20. Its current yield (TTM) is 5.18%. As investment class, international bonds are terrific for managing portfolio risk and the data from the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index bears that out.

While global bonds are not efficient at beating inflation (0.31 v. 0.40), they do perform well on average during down markets (3.52%). Meanwhile, they have a higher probability of generating a positive return (0.91 v. 0.47), beating the risk-free rate (0.53 v. 0.45), and controlling downside deviation (0.71 v. 0.62).

For those who are interested, I did mention that fixed income should always be part of the plan. In that piece, I suggested investing in First Trust Low Duration Opportunities ETF (XNAS: LMBS), iShares 7-10 Treasury Bond ETF (XNAS: IEF), and Van Eck Fallen Angels High Yield Bond ETF (XNAS: ANGL). If one had followed the advice from that piece, their fixed income allocation would have returned 1.43% since January. That includes the losses (2.99%) from ANGL. On the other hand, I do not recommend high-yield bonds as a haven against overall market losses. The data simply does not support that decision.

Gold

In November, I wrote that gold as part of an overall allocation was a great way to manage risk. While as a single investment, it does not outperform the S&P 500, it should still be part of one’s investment plan. If one had followed that advice, they would have seen the abrdn Physical Gold Shares ETF (ARCX: SGOL) increase in value by 10.66%. One can certainly see my data on last year’s piece, but there are three measures worth mentioning. Gold has averaged 8.34% (±21.26%) since 1972. During up markets it generates an average return of 9.50%. Where gold shines, pun intended, is what happens during down markets. While the S&P 500 falls an average of -12.81%, gold’s performance improves to an average of 13.07%. This asset class should always be part of one’s asset allocation. I still recommend a position up to 6% of one’s overall portfolio.

Hedge Funds

One of my followers suggested I call this class alternative investments, but I am using the same language as FactSet. This is my last recommendation to find havens of safety during market volatility. In November, I suggested allocating no more than 15% in ETFs that use hedging as a methodology. To review, this is how the Credit Suisse Hedge Index has performed since 1994.

In November, I recommended the First Trust Long/Short Equity ETF (ARCX: FTLS). Since that writing, it has outperformed the S&P 500 (6.43% v. -15.56%) and lost -9.67% since January 20.

I still like the fund. Since its inception in 2014, it has minimized losses to -0.48% (v. 14.92%) during down markets. During that same time, it has a higher probability of generating a positive return (0.89 v. 0.74), beating inflation (0.593 v. 0.589), and beating the risk-free rate (0.76 v. 0.74). The Sortino Ratios are similar at 1.10 v. 1.11. Since 2014, it has averaged 8.56% (v. 12.53%).

My Take

As I am writing this, we are in the middle of serious market correction. The Trump tariffs and his government reorganization has roiled the equity markets. This is still a fluid situation, and I am not sure when the free fall will end. I even dare to suggest that the traditional 60/40 portfolio should go to a 50/50 or 40/60 framework. Stashing money in less risky asset classes is not the worst idea and should be part of a normal strategy regardless. For review purposes, consider parking some money in these ETFs.
